roots.title: Ethics in Data Science and Property Tax Inequities
roots.summary: Dr. Edminson emphasizes the importance of ethics and human contexts in data science, particularly for Project A1 and A2, highlighting the Cook County Assessor's Office's open data initiative to improve property value assessments. Analysis reveals systemic inequities in property tax assessments, with lower-value homes often over-assessed, leading to regressive taxation that disproportionately affects lower-income homeowners. The discussion covers the financial impact of rising home appraisal values on low-income owners, racial discrimination in housing, and the historical context of housing inequality, including the effects of redlining and biased valuation methods.

children.title: Introduction to Ethics and Cook County Data Initiative
children.summary: Dr. Edminson discusses the significance of human contexts and ethics in data science, particularly for Project A1 and A2 assignments. The Cook County Assessor's Office's open data initiative aims to improve property value assessments for nearly 900,000 properties every three years, using recent sales data to address biases in the property tax system that impact marginalized communities.

children.title: Introduction to Ethics and Cook County Data Initiative
children.summary: Dr. Edminson, a UC Berkeley PhD, emphasizes the importance of human contexts and ethics in data science, particularly for upcoming Project A1 and A2 assignments. The Cook County Assessor's Office's open data initiative aims to enhance property value assessments using data science, highlighting the significance of accurate property appraisal for tax determination. The initiative assesses nearly 900,000 properties every three years, utilizing recent sales data, and will be analyzed through a case study that addresses biases in the property tax system affecting marginalized communities.

children.title: Introduction to Dr. Edminson and Course Ethics
children.summary: Audio issues were resolved before welcoming Dr. Edminson, a UC Berkeley PhD in history specializing in European philosophy and legal theory, who will contribute to the ethics component of the data science program. He introduced the topic of human contexts and ethics in data science, emphasizing its importance for upcoming Project A1 and A2 assignments and the need to consider ethical implications in model building with real-world data.

children.title: Introduction to Human Contexts and Ethics in Data Science
children.summary: Dr. Edminson introduces the topic of human contexts and ethics in data science, emphasizing its relevance to the upcoming projects A1 and A2. He aims to provide context and tools for understanding the complexities involved in building models with real-world data, highlighting the importance of considering ethical implications and the stakes of data science work.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Please join me in welcoming Dr. Edminson. Thank you for coming to our lecture. Okay, great. Thank you, Nargis, for the lovely introduction and thank you all for coming here today. So I can talk to you a little bit about... Well, on the one hand, the human context and ethics of data, the program that I help run and teach in here at UC Berkeley is part of the data science program. And also to give you some context and some tools to help you with your upcoming projects and two parts project A1 and A2. So um... Today, I'm going to tell you a little bit of a story. And in that story i'm gonna help you think through some stakes and context and give you some generalizable tools for thinking about The stakes and meaning of similar kinds of work when you're building models that do real work. in the real world. going to be working with the real world data set very soon and replicating the attempts of other data scientists to solve a real world problem. And I'm going to give you some of the context to help you think through what is it that I'm actually doing what should I be thinking about what should I be worrying about and why is the task maybe even more complicated than it seems at first glance. children.title: Cook County Assessor's Open Data Initiative Overview
children.summary: The Cook County Assessor's Office's open data initiative focuses on enhancing property value assessments through data science techniques to achieve fairer outcomes. This initiative is significant for social and political contexts, emphasizing the critical role of accurate property appraisal in tax determination. The case study will delve into the relevance and implications of this assessment process.

children.title: Cook County Assessor's Office: Open Data Initiative Overview
children.summary: The Cook County Assessor's Office's open data initiative aims to improve property value assessments using data science techniques for fairer outcomes. This initiative addresses a significant social and political issue, reflecting the importance of accurate property appraisal in determining taxes. The case study will explore the relevance and implications of this process.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, the story has to do with something called The Cook County Assessor's Office. and their open data initiative, which is actually the very source of the data that you're going to be working with in your project. And it has to do with the use of data science techniques to create more accurate, uniform, and in a simple, but telling translation, more fair assessments of property values. Now this might sound like something pretty distant from your everyday experience, so I'm going to do a little bit to talk to you about what this kind of process is, why it exists, why it's actually really important and plays a kind of vital social function that means it stakes are relatively high. So briefly, what is this case study about? You're looking at data and doing work that parallels the Cook County Assessor's office, this part of its open data initiative, which was created in response to a particular social and political problem that emerged at a specific moment in time. children.title: Cook County Assessor's Office: Property Valuation and Data Science
children.summary: The Cook County Assessor's Office assesses nearly 900,000 properties every three years to determine property taxes, employing techniques that leverage recent sales data for accuracy. This data-intensive process exemplifies the application of data science principles. A case study will analyze the problem addressed, the formulated response, and key takeaways, alongside lessons for data science practice. Additionally, the segment covers the historical context of open data initiatives, highlighting a 2017 report that exposed biases in the property tax system affecting marginalized communities.

children.title: Cook County Assessor's Office: Property Valuation
children.summary: The Cook County Assessor's Office in Illinois is responsible for assessing the value of nearly 900,000 residential and commercial properties every three years to determine property taxes. This process is significant in a large urban area like Chicago, where property tax assessment practices differ from those in states like California.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: is basically Chicago, Illinois, and some of the kind of suburbs and towns like Evanston right around Chicago. What is an assessor's office? Some of you might know, but a lot of you probably have never thought about this. You've never worried about paying property taxes, and if you're from California, property tax assessment works pretty different than it does in most other states, like Illinois. So what is an assessor's office do? It assesses property values at scale. That is, it tries to estimate how much is it any given property actually worth. And this means residential properties as well as commercial properties. And it has to do this every three years. And that's a really big task because in a big city, a big county like Chicago, there's quite a few properties. In Cook County, for example, there were nearly 900,000 assessed properties in its last cycle 2018 to 2021. Video: 1: Case Study: Cook County Assessor's Office and Property
Appraisal

children.title: Property Valuation and Tax Assessment Process
children.summary: The Cook County Assessor's Office assesses property values every three years to determine property taxes. They utilize techniques to estimate property values based on similarities to recently sold homes, especially when historical sales data is outdated, ensuring accurate tax assessments.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: They're just finishing the next cycle now. So you might be wondering why do they do this? Why are they guessing property values? You might be familiar with websites like Zillow or Redfin that are doing that that help you sort of decide, oh, how much is my property worth if I want to sell it? Or how much of the properties out there, you know, maybe worth if I'm looking to buy? Acesor's office do it for a very specific reason. They want to figure out how much property tax do you owe? That's ultimately what they're there for. They're there to figure out if you own property, you owe us taxes based on that property. And if your property hasn't sold in a really long time, they don't actually know. They can't just look at the last sales price because it might be decades old and a house that was sold for $50,000 in 1970, it could easily be worth a million dollars today. So they need techniques to help figure out based on the similarity of your home to other homes that have sold more recently. How much do we think that that home is worth and therefore how much in property taxes do you have to pay every single year? Video: 1: Case Study: Cook County Assessor's Office and Property
Appraisal

leaf_chunk.audio: They're just finishing the next cycle now. So you might be wondering why do they do this? Why are they guessing property values? You might be familiar with websites like Zillow or Redfin that are doing that that help you sort of decide, oh, how much is my property worth if I want to sell it? Or how much of the properties out there, you know, maybe worth if I'm looking to buy? Acesor's office do it for a very specific reason. They want to figure out how much property tax do you owe? That's ultimately what they're there for. They're there to figure out if you own property, you owe us taxes based on that property. And if your property hasn't sold in a really long time, they don't actually know. They can't just look at the last sales price because it might be decades old and a house that was sold for $50,000 in 1970, it could easily be worth a million dollars today. So they need techniques to help figure out based on the similarity of your home to other homes that have sold more recently. How much do we think that that home is worth and therefore how much in property taxes do you have to pay every single year?

children.title: Cook County Assessor's Office: Data Science in Property Tax
children.summary: The Cook County Assessor's Office is responsible for assessing property values every three years to determine property taxes. This process is data-intensive and exemplifies the application of data science techniques learned in the course.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: As I said, this is a massive operation. And at its core, this is... Data science work. And the techniques that you learn in classes like this one are very much utilized in actually kind of running these systems and it's been to property tax assessment has been really a data intensive practice for about a century now. children.title: Case Study Overview: Problem and Response
children.summary: The case study will be examined in four parts: identifying the problem it addresses, detailing the response formulated, understanding how it was framed, and summarizing key takeaways. Additionally, there will be a review of lessons for data science practice, outlining questions relevant to each stage of the data science life cycle.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So today, I'm going to examine this case study. that you're going to be replicating and walking through in your project in about four parts. We'll be looking at what was the problem precisely that this is responding to. What was the problem that this was responding to? What was the response to it? How was it formulated? How was it understood? How was it framed? And then all of the very un- briefly run through just kind of review what are some of the key takeaways like what was the point of all of this. And then at the end I won't walk through it but there's a set of slides on you know lessons for data science practice that walk you through sort of the kinds of questions that you can ask each stage of the data science life cycle that you've learned about in this class is a kind of normative framework for what is. children.title: Historical Context of Open Data Initiatives
children.summary: The segment discusses the historical and social context that led to the creation of an open data initiative, stemming from a 2017 investigative report by the Chicago Tribune. This report revealed systematic bias and corruption in the Cook County Assessor's Office, resulting in a regressive property tax system that disproportionately affected lower-class and black homeowners in Chicago.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: data science, you know, what is the, you know, your actual activities as a data scientist look like. And it'll be basically giving you added questions. What else can you be asking about related to the human context and ethics of data science practice at each of those stages. So those slides will be just a resource at the end to kind of take with you as you kind of continue in in this course. So let's start with the problem, the historical and social context that actually led to the creation of an open data initiative that led to the creation and making public of the data that you work within your project. That's where we got the data from when we built this project. It starts with a 2017 investigative report from the Chicago Tribune, which uncovered... and precipitated a major scandal in the property assessment system managed by the Cook County Assessor's Office under the watch of its now former County Assessor Joseph Barrios. Working with experts from the University of Chicago, experts in property assessment and data science, journalists that demonstrate the presence of systematic bias and corruption that resulted in a highly regressive tax system. A regressive tax system means one where the disproportionate burden of the taxes fell on lower class and particularly black homeowners in a Chicago area. Video: 1: Ari Edmundson

leaf_chunk.audio: data science, you know, what is the, you know, your actual activities as a data scientist look like. And it'll be basically giving you added questions. What else can you be asking about related to the human context and ethics of data science practice at each of those stages. So those slides will be just a resource at the end to kind of take with you as you kind of continue in in this course. So let's start with the problem, the historical and social context that actually led to the creation of an open data initiative that led to the creation and making public of the data that you work within your project. That's where we got the data from when we built this project. It starts with a 2017 investigative report from the Chicago Tribune, which uncovered... and precipitated a major scandal in the property assessment system managed by the Cook County Assessor's Office under the watch of its now former County Assessor Joseph Barrios. Working with experts from the University of Chicago, experts in property assessment and data science, journalists that demonstrate the presence of systematic bias and corruption that resulted in a highly regressive tax system. A regressive tax system means one where the disproportionate burden of the taxes fell on lower class and particularly black homeowners in a Chicago area.

children.title: Property Tax Assessment: Inequities and Disparities
children.summary: Data analysis shows systemic inequities in property tax assessments, with lower-value homes over-assessed, leading to higher tax rates for lower-income homeowners and a regressive taxation system. Metrics like the coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential highlight these disparities, supported by Cook County graphs. The discussion includes the impact of the 2008 recession on property assessments, inaccuracies in valuation models, discriminatory tax burdens, and the influence of algorithmic bias and demographic factors in the appeals process.

children.title: Property Tax Assessment: Inequities and Regressivity
children.summary: Data analysis reveals systemic inequities in property tax assessments, with lower-value homes over-assessed, resulting in higher tax rates for lower-income homeowners and a regressive taxation scheme. Metrics such as the coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential demonstrate these disparities, supported by graphs from Cook County that illustrate the regressivity of property tax rates across various quintiles of property value and household income.

children.title: Property Tax Assessment: Inequities and Regressivity
children.summary: Data analysis shows systemic inequities in property tax assessments, with lower-value homes being over-assessed, leading to higher tax rates for lower-income homeowners and creating a regressive taxation scheme. Metrics like the coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential highlight these disparities, while graphs from Cook County illustrate the regressivity of property tax rates across different quintiles of property value and household income.

children.title: Property Tax Assessment: Systemic Inequities
children.summary: Data analysis revealed that property tax assessments are systematically skewed, leading to inaccuracies where lower-value homes are over-assessed, resulting in disproportionately high tax rates for lower-income homeowners. This creates a regressive taxation scheme, contrary to the intended fairness of a flat tax rate based on property value. Metrics such as the coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential were discussed to illustrate these disparities.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And is part of this kind of journalistic effort they leveraged. Data analysis and visualization. To help make visible the workings of what they were arguing was a deeply unjust and unfair system. They demonstrated with reference to some standard metrics that have been developed in the property assessment business, that the system was not only generally inaccurate, that is across the board, across different property values, it was kind of well off the mark, but more than that, that the errors had a sort of structural tendency. They were distributed systematically in a particular way. Where is property taxes are supposed to be a flat rate that is everybody pays the same percentage of their property values of the property value. So you have a thousand dollar house you're paying say 8% or not a point like 0.8% a million dollar house you're paying 0.8%. The form of fairness that's built into the system is imagine to be you pay an amount of taxes proportional to your home value. And so when the errors are are systematically skewed and say you're a homeowner with a home of a lesser value and your house is being over assessed. You're going to be paying a kind of disproportionately high share of taxes if the people who have more expensive homes are being under assessed and therefore paying less than their fair share. So that would be the difference between, say, on the one hand a regressive taxation scheme. That is the poor lower income home owners pay effectively a higher tax rate than high value property owners. Video: 1: As income l

leaf_chunk.audio: And is part of this kind of journalistic effort they leveraged. Data analysis and visualization. To help make visible the workings of what they were arguing was a deeply unjust and unfair system. They demonstrated with reference to some standard metrics that have been developed in the property assessment business, that the system was not only generally inaccurate, that is across the board, across different property values, it was kind of well off the mark, but more than that, that the errors had a sort of structural tendency. They were distributed systematically in a particular way. Where is property taxes are supposed to be a flat rate that is everybody pays the same percentage of their property values of the property value. So you have a thousand dollar house you're paying say 8% or not a point like 0.8% a million dollar house you're paying 0.8%. The form of fairness that's built into the system is imagine to be you pay an amount of taxes proportional to your home value. And so when the errors are are systematically skewed and say you're a homeowner with a home of a lesser value and your house is being over assessed. You're going to be paying a kind of disproportionately high share of taxes if the people who have more expensive homes are being under assessed and therefore paying less than their fair share. So that would be the difference between, say, on the one hand a regressive taxation scheme. That is the poor lower income home owners pay effectively a higher tax rate than high value property owners.

children.title: Property Taxation: Regressivity and Effective Rates
children.summary: Progressive taxation schemes aim to redistribute wealth by taxing higher earners at higher rates, while property taxation is intended to be flat, with a single effective tax rate. Graphs illustrate the regressivity of property tax rates in Cook County, examining the impact on different quintiles of property value and household income, highlighting the disparities in tax burdens.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And that would be compared to say a progressive taxation scheme progressive taxation schemes are often called redistributive they tax higher earners or those of higher property values at a higher rate and redistribute basically is a way of redistributing and trying to equalize unequal systems. But in property taxation, it's not supposed to be regressive or progressive. It's supposed to be flat. Yes. That's a property tax rate. So effective tax rate there means it's supposed to be one single tax rate. It should be that that line should be flat. And that says, what are people actually paying in practice on average across these different median household incomes? Yeah. Great question. So these graphs are basically trying to demonstrate that. That's a graph illustrating the regressivity of the effective property tax rate in Cook County with respect not just to the property value, but with respect, you know, which other graph studies that like, you know, for each quintile of property value, you know, zero to $100,000, $100, you know, whatever. leaf_chunk.audio: And that would be compared to say a progressive taxation scheme progressive taxation schemes are often called redistributive they tax higher earners or those of higher property values at a higher rate and redistribute basically is a way of redistributing and trying to equalize unequal systems. But in property taxation, it's not supposed to be regressive or progressive. It's supposed to be flat. Yes. That's a property tax rate. So effective tax rate there means it's supposed to be one single tax rate. It should be that that line should be flat. And that says, what are people actually paying in practice on average across these different median household incomes? Yeah. Great question. So these graphs are basically trying to demonstrate that. That's a graph illustrating the regressivity of the effective property tax rate in Cook County with respect not just to the property value, but with respect, you know, which other graph studies that like, you know, for each quintile of property value, you know, zero to $100,000, $100, you know, whatever. It's about, you know, household income. So different ways of measuring and trying to visualize who exactly is this harming.

children.title: Income and Racial Disparities in Property Taxation
children.summary: The lecture discusses the regressive nature of the property tax system, which disproportionately impacts lower-income homeowners and highlights income disparities among property owners. It examines the effects of the 2008 recession on property assessments, revealing inaccuracies in valuation models, and addresses discriminatory tax burdens in Cook County, including racial discrimination claims. The role of algorithmic bias and demographic factors in the appeals process for property assessments is also analyzed.

children.title: Income and Racial Disparities in Property Taxation
children.summary: The lecture covers the regressive nature of the property tax system, disproportionately affecting lower-income homeowners and highlighting income disparities among property owners. It discusses the impact of the 2008 recession on property assessments, revealing inaccuracies in valuation models, and addresses discriminatory tax burdens in Cook County, including racial discrimination claims. Additionally, the role of algorithmic bias and demographic factors in the appeals process for property assessments is examined.

children.title: Impact of Income on Property Tax Burdens
children.summary: The tax system is regressive, imposing heavier burdens on lower-income homeowners and violating the principle of a flat tax rate. Mapping property values to income reveals that some low-valued homes are owned by landlords with higher incomes, highlighting the need to understand who is affected by these tax policies. Additionally, effective tax rates and standards of accuracy and fairness are discussed, emphasizing the disparities across different communities in Cook County.

children.title: Impact of 2008 Recession on Property Assessments
children.summary: The recession starting in 2008 led to a significant drop in property values, which was not accurately reflected in assessment models that relied on past sales data. While disparities in property assessments were noted in Cook County compared to other jurisdictions, the issues were not solely due to the recession, indicating additional underlying factors affecting property valuations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What caused this? Well, some of this was the effects of the recession starting in 2008. That basically their models hadn't caught up with. Property values plummeted after 2008, but people kept being assessed higher based on past sales data. And this just they didn't fully catch up to it. But it wasn't solely that because compared to the analysis showed that other jurisdictions around the US that also would experience big plummets and overall property values didn't have the same kind of disparity. Most jurisdictions actually do our regressive to some degree and have disparity, but in Cook County it was particularly stark. And clearly there was something else going on here that simply the effects of the recession. Video: 1: As income I

children.title: Discriminatory Tax Burdens in Cook County
children.summary: A lawsuit highlighted that the tax system in Cook County imposes an unfair burden on property owners, not only based on income and property value but also racially. The plaintiffs argued that this discrimination violates anti-discrimination clauses of the civil rights acts. Data presented included effective tax rates and assessments showing disparities across different racial demographics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Another graph that came out of a lawsuit that was subsequent to this revelation that was leveraged by a former by property owners against the assessor burials demonstrated the unfair burden of burials as tax system wasn't only just regressive based on on property value or on income but also in terms of race and they brought up a lawsuits in addition to actually say that this isn't just unfair because of class differences but it's discriminatory against a legally protected class and therefore they're arguing it violates anti-discrimination clauses that have come out of the civil rights acts So It's clear there was a problem, what caused it. children.title: Algorithmic Bias in Property Assessment Systems
children.summary: The discussion highlights that the issue in Cook County is not solely due to a biased algorithm but rather the social, political, and technical arrangements of the property assessment system. Wealthy homeowners can successfully challenge their property assessments through an appeal process, which involves human oversight, contrasting with typical criticisms of algorithmic bias that advocate for human intervention to address issues.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: They said it's not just the recession. You might also be thinking maybe you've taken data 104 or another HCE class or you've just been generally aware of ethics of data science and you might be thinking, oh, this must be a case of a biased algorithm, an algorithm that is learned something about the world and the way that racism works and it's reproducing racism kind of directly from it. Well, that's not exactly the case here. Now this is a slightly different case than classical. instances of algorithmic bias. It wasn't in the main the issue of a faulty model that created a more aggressive system. Although that's a little bit of the case in general if you use a really simple linear model it will tend to be regressive because it regresses to the mean that they regress towards the mean price of lower valued homes will tend to be overestimated. But it's very easy and possible to build a model that's not such a simple linear model. So that was one piece of the puzzle but not the entirety of it. The biggest. cause that marked out the distinctiveness of the disparity in Cook County. Was actually because of the particular social, political, and technical arrangements of the property assessment system. That is one that enabled the wealthy and the more privileged. to successfully challenge their assessments. Basically this is what I mean, this is how it works. Every household in Cook County gets an assessment mail to them. It tells them this is how much we think your house is worth and therefore this is how much in property taxes you're going to be paying for the next three years. If you the homeowner believed that you've been over assessed you think that they got something wrong. You can actually go and make an appeal. There's a human in the loop. It's precisely the kind of thing that critics of algorithmic bias say you need to have to fix a problem. Put a human in the loop. Make there a review board that can actually you can go be it before and say hey I think your algorithm made a mistake. But in fact, it's precisely that institution that was part of the problem here. So you go to a review board which is made up of elected officials and you go in and you make your case. It's a way of trying to build in public confidence and accountability into the system. Sure, air is happened but you can go and try to get them fixed. Video: 1: Neighborhood

leaf_chunk.audio: They said it's not just the recession. You might also be thinking maybe you've taken data 104 or another HCE class or you've just been generally aware of ethics of data science and you might be thinking, oh, this must be a case of a biased algorithm, an algorithm that is learned something about the world and the way that racism works and it's reproducing racism kind of directly from it. Well, that's not exactly the case here. Now this is a slightly different case than classical. instances of algorithmic bias. It wasn't in the main the issue of a faulty model that created a more aggressive system. Although that's a little bit of the case in general if you use a really simple linear model it will tend to be regressive because it regresses to the mean that they regress towards the mean price of lower valued homes will tend to be overestimated. But it's very easy and possible to build a model that's not such a simple linear model. So that was one piece of the puzzle but not the entirety of it. The biggest. cause that marked out the distinctiveness of the disparity in Cook County. Was actually because of the particular social, political, and technical arrangements of the property assessment system. That is one that enabled the wealthy and the more privileged. to successfully challenge their assessments. Basically this is what I mean, this is how it works. Every household in Cook County gets an assessment mail to them. It tells them this is how much we think your house is worth and therefore this is how much in property taxes you're going to be paying for the next three years. If you the homeowner believed that you've been over assessed you think that they got something wrong. You can actually go and make an appeal. There's a human in the loop. It's precisely the kind of thing that critics of algorithmic bias say you need to have to fix a problem. Put a human in the loop. Make there a review board that can actually you can go be it before and say hey I think your algorithm made a mistake. But in fact, it's precisely that institution that was part of the problem here. So you go to a review board which is made up of elected officials and you go in and you make your case. It's a way of trying to build in public confidence and accountability into the system. Sure, air is happened but you can go and try to get them fixed.

children.title: Impact of Demographics on Appeals Process
children.summary: The discussion highlights how the appeals process in property assessments can be problematic, particularly through the lens of critical race theory and critical legal theory. It emphasizes that institutions perceived as neutral may perpetuate issues, illustrated by data on homeowner demographics and appeal rates from 2011-2015, showing correlations between income levels and racial composition with appeal outcomes.

children.title: Impact of Anti-Discrimination Laws on Inequality
children.summary: Anti-discrimination has been defined since the 1970s as ensuring equal access to institutions, which theoretically prevents formal discrimination. However, this approach can obscure and perpetuate underlying inequalities, particularly evident in the context of formally impartial algorithms discussed in case studies within the HC curriculum. A related analysis of homeowner demographics and appeal rates from 2011-2015 highlights how median income and racial composition influence property valuation disputes.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Um. Which is basically the way that anti-discrimination has come to be defined since the 1970s. If everybody has ultimately the same access to an institution, the law doesn't actually formally discriminate. Then in theory, you're okay. What happens is those sorts of systems tend to obscure underlying inequalities. that they can then perpetuate. And so this is true of formally impartial algorithms, which is what you learn in other case studies in the HC curriculum. Video: 1: Neighborhood

children.title: Inequality in Tax Assessment Appeals
children.summary: Access to appeal tax assessments is formally equal, but practical participation is unequal due to socioeconomic factors. Those with financial means can afford to take time off work and hire tax lawyers, leading to disparities in success rates among different demographics, as illustrated by data on homeowners and appeal rates from 2011-2015.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So why in this case would this be an unequal institution? Because although everybody formally has the same access to go and appeal their tax assessments, everybody can in theory go, there's no law that says you have to have a certain amount of money to actually go and appeal your assessment. But only those that actually have the means to take time off work to go do it, that have means to hire tax lawyers who know the intricacies of the system would actually go and be able to find success. children.title: Property Tax Appeals and Inequity in Chicago
children.summary: Chicago has a significant tax attorney industry focused on appealing property assessments, with a notable increase in appeals in Cook County. In 2015, residential property owners filed appeals for 370,000 parcels, achieving reductions 80% of the time, highlighting disparities in success rates based on wealth and race. Analysis indicates that while appeals are intended to promote fairness, systemic issues lead to unequal access to this process.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In fact, Chicago boasts a very large and thriving tax attorney industry dedicated precisely to appealing property assessments, which is reflected in the growing number of appeals in Cook County across the first decades of the 21st century, which is one of the things that you see here. If you would go drive around especially like eight years ago, drive around Chicago, six to eight years ago, and even still today, you would see billboards advertising law firms that were dedicated to going and getting you property tax reductions. It was just a huge part of the kind of lawyer industry in Chicago. often. And we're more successful in winning reductions, much more often than they're less wealthy and less white, neighbors. And so just for an example in 2015 alone, residential property owners filed assessment appeals involving 370,000 parcels and one reduction's 80% of the time. Now the Tribune's analysis pointed towards corruption, and lack of transparency is the root of the problem. Video: 1: Neighborhood

children.title: Influence of Tax Law Firms on Illinois Politics
children.summary: The close ties between assessors and tax law firms raise concerns about fairness in property appeals, with significant campaign contributions influencing outcomes. The narrative of community engagement is often a facade, masking deeper systemic issues and potential corruption. It is crucial to critically evaluate claims of fairness in both social systems and technology, relying on empirical evidence rather than promotional language.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The assessor, Barrios, had close, very close ties to this tax lawyer industry, more than half of his campaign contributions came from these tax law firms that stood to benefit and were benefiting considerably from the system. The chief partner of the biggest law firm dealing with the largest property appeals was also an Illinois the Illinois house to speaker. Industry plays a powerful role in Illinois in Chicago politics. And they cleverly adapt to the language of community engagement, keeping a human in the loop, to give the system a veneer of fairness. And so there's a takeaway here that applies both to a system like real social systems as well as to kind of claims about algorithms. Don't be fooled by this kind of ethics washing. You know, you see the language here coming from the deputy assessor trying to make you say, tell you that this system is clearly the best and and fairest way to do it. You always have to look at the details at the way that a system actually works in practice and not the way that it gets advertised. This is true of the way tech firms will describe and talk about the fairness of their own systems, about their inclusivity, actually look at what they do in practice, which takes empirical work by social scientists. leaf_chunk.audio: The assessor, Barrios, had close, very close ties to this tax lawyer industry, more than half of his campaign contributions came from these tax law firms that stood to benefit and were benefiting considerably from the system. The chief partner of the biggest law firm dealing with the largest property appeals was also an Illinois the Illinois house to speaker. Industry plays a powerful role in Illinois in Chicago politics. And they cleverly adapt to the language of community engagement, keeping a human in the loop, to give the system a veneer of fairness. And so there's a takeaway here that applies both to a system like real social systems as well as to kind of claims about algorithms. Don't be fooled by this kind of ethics washing. You know, you see the language here coming from the deputy assessor trying to make you say, tell you that this system is clearly the best and and fairest way to do it. You always have to look at the details at the way that a system actually works in practice and not the way that it gets advertised. This is true of the way tech firms will describe and talk about the fairness of their own systems, about their inclusivity, actually look at what they do in practice, which takes empirical work by social scientists.

children.title: Home Appraisal Impact on Low-Income Owners
children.summary: A rise in home appraisal values can financially burden low-income homeowners, increasing the risk of foreclosure or forced sales due to higher payments. Systematic issues in property valuation influence tax assessments, particularly in Cook County, creating a zero-sum scenario. The discussion also addresses racial discrimination in housing, illustrating systemic inequities through the experiences of undervalued homeowners.

children.title: Home Appraisal Effects on Low-Income Owners
children.summary: A sudden rise in home appraisal values can financially burden low-income homeowners, leading to risks of foreclosure or forced sales due to increased payments. Systematic issues in property valuation affect tax assessments, with individual taxes in Cook County tied to overall valuations, creating a zero-sum scenario. The segment also discusses racial discrimination in housing, highlighting systemic inequities through a homeowner's experience with undervaluation.

children.title: Home Appraisal Impact on Low-Income Owners and Taxes
children.summary: A sudden increase in home appraisal values can financially strain low-income homeowners, risking foreclosure or forced sale due to higher payments. Systematic property valuation issues affect tax assessments, as individual property taxes in Cook County are influenced by overall property valuations, creating a zero-sum scenario. Additionally, the segment addresses racial discrimination in housing practices, highlighting systemic inequities exemplified by a homeowner's experience with undervaluation.

children.title: Impact of Home Appraisal on Low-Income Homeowners
children.summary: A sudden increase in home appraisal values can lead to severe financial strain for low-income homeowners, potentially resulting in foreclosure or forced sale. Homeowners may find themselves responsible for significantly higher payments than initially budgeted, which can jeopardize their ability to maintain home ownership.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: one year only to suddenly find that the house was appraised at levels far in excess of what they paid for it. Suddenly threatening them with the possibility of no longer being able to afford their home to pay off the mortgage. This can result in drastic consequences. This is not just an inconvenience if you're a low income homeowner. It can result in you having to lose your house, having a foreclosed-on, or having to sell it because you can no longer afford it, because you're suddenly responsible for paying thousands of dollars more every year than you had budgeted. And suddenly you just don't have the means to handle it. Video: 1: Human impacts

children.title: Impact of Property Valuation on Taxes
children.summary: Systematic overvaluation or undervaluation of properties in a neighborhood affects the valuation of similar properties, leading to skewed assessments. The fixed property tax collection in Cook County means that individual property taxes are determined based on the overall assessed value, creating a zero-sum game where one property's tax is influenced by others' valuations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is not just a problem for individuals. Because what happens when a house or a group of houses in a neighborhood are systematically overvalued. or other properties are undervalued. In other words, when air rates are distributed so that Air SKUs positive for lower valued homes and SKUs negative for higher valued homes. Well, several things happens. Because it's all connected and relational. Other similar properties, those in their own neighborhood that have similar features to them, are going to start being more likely to be overvalued in the model, because those features become more heavily weighted in that model than before. amount of taxes set to be collected in Chicago is fixed. Like basically the county set says we're going to collect this much in property taxes and assessment is not telling you exactly how much you're going to pay because the percentage is not fixed. They basically say here's how much we want to collect and then let's figure out exactly how much all the property value is worth and then each individual property gets you pay based on basically what fraction of that overall hole your property is worth. So if all the value of all the properties in Cook County is estimated to say to be a hundred billion dollars and they love you a one percent tax on every property. then They basically have to break up that. The one the one percent comes from their determination that they would need to collect a billion dollars sorry didn't phrase that clearly they basically say we need a billion dollars in property taxes if they find out that it's all worth a hundred billion dollars all the properties. Then they say okay we're levying a one percent tax rate on on on everybody. But what happens because this basically means that it's a zero-sum game. Every properties were, every properties amount of tax is related to how much other people are paying. So even if your home is not being, say, undervalued relative to its actual value. or overvalued relative to an its actual value if everybody with more expensive homes is being undervalued even if the assessment of your house is correct you will end up paying more. So it's actually the way that the whole system works that translates into how much am I going to be paid. This is also, by the way, true of the relationship between residential and commercial properties as well. I'm you're only going to be working with residential properties in your project, but basically the kind of slice of the pie is also divided up between all the residential properties and all the commercial properties. So commercial properties are undervalued as they really were, then residential properties by default end up having to paint more. Video: 1: Human impacts

children.title: Racial Housing Inequality: Historical Context and Valuation
children.summary: Regressive taxation and historical racist housing practices, including redlining, have led to significant racial housing inequality in the U.S. Urban segregation was exacerbated during the Great Migration, with federal policies limiting mortgage access for African Americans. Valuation methods from the 1920s and 1930s incorporated race into property assessments, perpetuating disparities, which the Cook County Assessor's Office aims to address through a Data Science Initiative focusing on fair assessment algorithms.

children.title: Racial Housing Inequality: Historical Context and Valuation
children.summary: Regressive taxation systems and historical racist housing practices, such as redlining, have significantly contributed to racial housing inequality in the U.S. Urban segregation emerged during the Great Migration, with federal housing policies restricting mortgage access for African Americans. Real estate valuation methods developed in the 1920s and 1930s encoded race into property assessments, perpetuating market disparities. The Cook County Assessor's Office is addressing these issues through a Data Science Initiative aimed at reforming assessment practices and developing fair algorithms for home value assessments.

children.title: Historical Context of Racial Housing Inequality
children.summary: Regressive taxation systems disproportionately affect low-income homeowners, contributing to significant disparities in housing. A historical overview highlights how racist housing practices, particularly redlining, have driven racial inequality in the U.S., restricting mortgage access based on racial composition. Post-Civil War, housing became a key factor in structural racism, with segregationist policies in the Jim Crow South and the Civil Rights Act's role in dismantling these laws.

children.title: Historical Context of Racial Housing Inequality
children.summary: Regressive taxation systems disproportionately impact low-income homeowners, leading to significant disparities. A brief overview of the history of racist housing practices is provided to contextualize the urgency of issues uncovered by the Chicago Tribune, particularly focusing on how housing has driven racial inequality in the U.S., including practices like redlining that restricted access to mortgages based on racial composition.

children.title: Housing Practices and Racial Inequality
children.summary: Housing has historically been a major driver of structural racism and racial inequality in the United States, particularly since the Civil War. Segregationist policies, especially in the Jim Crow South, enforced racial separation in public facilities, while the Civil Rights Act helped to dismantle these laws. Additionally, practices like redlining made it difficult for certain racial groups to obtain federally-backed mortgages, labeling neighborhoods as 'risky' based on their racial composition.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It was part of the whole and deep history and pattern of driving inequality through housing practices. Housing in real estate has been one of the most significant in enduring drivers of structural racism and racial inequality in the history of the United States, going back to the aftermath of the Civil War. So many of you are likely familiar with some effective segregationist policies in the United States in the 20th century, particularly in the Jim Crow South, which forbid African Americans from using the same facilities as whites, buses, bathrooms, and pools. Maybe you've learned about the role of the Civil Rights Act or the Civil Rights Movement in basically rendering that illegal. Video: 1: Real estate and racial

children.title: Urban Segregation, Redlining, and Racial Valuation
children.summary: Urban neighborhoods in the North became segregated through housing practices despite the absence of Jim Crow laws, particularly during the Great Migration. Redlining, initiated by federal housing agencies, restricted mortgage access for African Americans and has lasting demographic impacts. The origins of redlining involved early data science techniques in real estate, where racial factors were coded into property valuation algorithms, significantly affecting home values.

children.title: Urban Segregation and Housing Practices
children.summary: Neighborhoods in the urban North, lacking Jim Crow laws, became increasingly segregated through subtle practices, particularly in housing, driven by the private real estate industry and government connections. Segregation intensified during the Great Migration of African Americans to urban centers, where although government-mandated segregation was illegal, other social practices and facially neutral laws facilitated similar outcomes.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But this was also a story simultaneously of how neighborhoods in the urban north that didn't have the same Jim Crow laws became increasingly segregated through sets of other more subtle forms of practices. In some cases in the case of housing it was driven by private real estate industry and connection with government actors. So, is segregation increased across the first half of the 20th century as part of the two ways of the Great Migration of African Americans from the South into the urban centers of the North? And as those urban centers received them, they developed practices for segregating. Although it was illegal for governments to actively segregate neighborhoods according to race, to use that as a good reason that you can or can't live in a certain area, other methods were available for essentially achieving the same ends in practice. So again, facial neutral laws, but there were other sorts of social practices that enabled that segregation to take place anyways. Video: 1: Real estate and racial

leaf_chunk.audio: But this was also a story simultaneously of how neighborhoods in the urban north that didn't have the same Jim Crow laws became increasingly segregated through sets of other more subtle forms of practices. In some cases in the case of housing it was driven by private real estate industry and connection with government actors. So, is segregation increased across the first half of the 20th century as part of the two ways of the Great Migration of African Americans from the South into the urban centers of the North? And as those urban centers received them, they developed practices for segregating. Although it was illegal for governments to actively segregate neighborhoods according to race, to use that as a good reason that you can or can't live in a certain area, other methods were available for essentially achieving the same ends in practice. So again, facial neutral laws, but there were other sorts of social practices that enabled that segregation to take place anyways.

children.title: Redlining and its Impact on Racial Inequality
children.summary: Redlining, a practice initiated by federal housing agencies during the Great Depression, involved mapping neighborhoods by risk to restrict mortgage access, particularly affecting African Americans. Areas marked as high risk were predominantly black and faced significant barriers to home ownership, a legacy that continues to impact demographics today, as evidenced by mapping redlining patterns onto current demographics in cities like Berkeley and Chicago.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One of the most notorious practices was what has come to be known is redlining. Drawing maps of cities that distinguish neighborhoods in terms of relative risk. Redlining was performed by federal housing agencies during the Great Depression in the 1930s in order to increase home ownership for lower-income Americans, or at least white-lower-income Americans. The areas coded as highest risk, colored in red, hence the name of redlining, were deemed too risky for the federal government to ensure banks issuing mortgages in those areas. So it became especially difficult to access loans. and buy a home. As these neighborhoods were disproportionately black, this meant that credit was near impossible to come by for African Americans looking to own a house. And the effects of these practices has continued to resonate today. And in a number of jurisdictions, including in the Bay Area and in Berkeley and Chicago, if you look at redlining masks, you can actually map them on to demographics today with a surprising amount of fidelity. children.title: Origins of Redlining and Data Practices
children.summary: Redlining originated not just from government actions but also from early data science techniques used by the real estate industry in the 1920s. Real estate professionals aimed to standardize property valuation methods, creating manuals for appraising homes with empirical rigor, which contributed to the federal housing system and the establishment of redlining maps.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Now the origins of this practice of redlining, it wasn't simply a top-down government initiative to racially segregate. We're actually lay in the data that the federal government was using. And that came out of a change in data practices, basically early data science techniques, developed by the private real estate industry in the 1920s. In other words, segregation was established and reinforced in part through the work of real estate professionals who were concerned with establishing reliable methods for predicting the value of a home that it was part of professionalizing a certain kind of data science practice is and and generating new forms of making data that actually underwrote the federal housing system that was captured in these redlining maps. And again, was this the real estate industry was professionalized in a few places in this, uh, in a, uh, at first one of those places was not accidentally Chicago in the first half of the 20th century. The men that were part of these industries aimed to standardize procedures for assessing property values, that is to make more objective, precise, accurate mathematical, the determination of like, what do you think a home is worth so we can go and better sell it? They wanted property appraisal to be a kind of empirical and rigorous data science guided by strict methods and principles. Beginning in the 1920s, these burgeoning professionals, professionals in places like Chicago and Cleveland began creating manuals for training real estate assessors in the proper scientific methods for appraising property. leaf_chunk.audio: Now the origins of this practice of redlining, it wasn't simply a top-down government initiative to racially segregate. We're actually lay in the data that the federal government was using. And that came out of a change in data practices, basically early data science techniques, developed by the private real estate industry in the 1920s. In other words, segregation was established and reinforced in part through the work of real estate professionals who were concerned with establishing reliable methods for predicting the value of a home that it was part of professionalizing a certain kind of data science practice is and and generating new forms of making data that actually underwrote the federal housing system that was captured in these redlining maps. And again, was this the real estate industry was professionalized in a few places in this, uh, in a, uh, at first one of those places was not accidentally Chicago in the first half of the 20th century. The men that were part of these industries aimed to standardize procedures for assessing property values, that is to make more objective, precise, accurate mathematical, the determination of like, what do you think a home is worth so we can go and better sell it? They wanted property appraisal to be a kind of empirical and rigorous data science guided by strict methods and principles. Beginning in the 1920s, these burgeoning professionals, professionals in places like Chicago and Cleveland began creating manuals for training real estate assessors in the proper scientific methods for appraising property.

children.title: Real estate valuation and racial factors
children.summary: Race was directly coded into algorithms used for mapping property values, with racial and foreign neighbors identified as significant factors that could decrease a home's value. A 1937 appraisal manual highlighted various deductions affecting property valuation, including up to 60% for racial and foreign neighbors, illustrating how these biases were institutionalized in real estate practices.

children.title: Real estate valuation and racial inequality
children.summary: Federal policies and real estate professionals in the 1920s and 1930s developed valuation methods that encoded race as a factor, influencing market practices and federal policies. A 1937 Appraising Manual illustrates deductions in property value based on racial composition and other factors. The historical system of racial inequality continues to affect housing, with black homeowners facing dual pressures of loan risk perception and potential overvaluation by tax assessors.

children.title: Real estate, racial inequality, and valuation methods
children.summary: Segregation in the U.S. was shaped by federal policies and real estate professionals who developed a 'scientific' approach to property valuation in the 1920s and 1930s. This involved creating objective rating systems that encoded race as a factor in property value assessments, influencing both market practices and federal policies. A table from a 1937 Appraising Manual highlights common deductions from property value based on factors such as racial composition, neighborhood obsolescence, and proximity to nuisances.

children.title: Historical context of racial inequality in housing
children.summary: The discussion highlights how a historical system of racial inequality and segregation continues to impact housing today. It notes that while the racial data revolution is not the direct cause of overassessment of black homeowners' properties in Cook County, housing has been a significant vector of racial inequality. Homeowners face dual pressures of being seen as risky for loans while also dealing with potential overvaluation by tax assessors, leading to financial strain.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this all contributed to an evolving and self-reinforcing system of racial inequality and segregation that's still alive today. Now I do want to note that this racial data revolution is not directly responsible for the overassessment of black homer's property we see in Cook County today. It would benefit owners of lower-valued properties to have their homes be undervalued because that would mean paying lower property tax. So it's not because of, say, proxies that are still in the data that these homes are being overvalued as we've said this comes from other places. The point of running through this history is not to tell you this is how you get bad or biased data that generates bad algorithms. It's rather to give you a historical background to explain how housing has been a major vector of racial inequality for the last century. you seen as risky on the one hand, which makes it hard to get loans to buy homes, and on the other restricting access to credit. So that's all on one hand. And on the other, you're caught in the opposite direction. The home can be overvalued by the tax assessors, so you're paying a higher, a higher price. So for those homeowners that are caught between this, you're getting squeezed in both directions. Video: 1: Real estate and racial inequality in the United States

leaf_chunk.audio: So this all contributed to an evolving and self-reinforcing system of racial inequality and segregation that's still alive today. Now I do want to note that this racial data revolution is not directly responsible for the overassessment of black homer's property we see in Cook County today. It would benefit owners of lower-valued properties to have their homes be undervalued because that would mean paying lower property tax. So it's not because of, say, proxies that are still in the data that these homes are being overvalued as we've said this comes from other places. The point of running through this history is not to tell you this is how you get bad or biased data that generates bad algorithms. It's rather to give you a historical background to explain how housing has been a major vector of racial inequality for the last century. you seen as risky on the one hand, which makes it hard to get loans to buy homes, and on the other restricting access to credit. So that's all on one hand. And on the other, you're caught in the opposite direction. The home can be overvalued by the tax assessors, so you're paying a higher, a higher price. So for those homeowners that are caught between this, you're getting squeezed in both directions.

children.title: Cook County Assessor's Data Science Initiative
children.summary: The Cook County Assessor's Office has launched a Data Science Initiative to reform assessment practices following public outcry. The data science lifecycle emphasizes problem formulation and the importance of context, with a focus on creating fair algorithms for home value assessments. The project aims to develop a system that accurately predicts home sale prices and operates fairly across racial and income lines, addressing social implications of assessment inaccuracies.

children.title: Cook County Assessor's Office: Data Science Initiative
children.summary: Following public outrage over the previous assessor's practices, a new assessor was elected in Cook County with a mandate to reform the system. This included the establishment of a data science office and an open data initiative aimed at creating a fairer and more accurate algorithmic model. The focus will be on understanding the data science life cycle and the importance of defining precise tasks and understanding model impacts.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, that's the background, that's the problem. Where does the data come from? How do they intend to use it and how is it supposed to fix this problem? the rest of today's class. So the new assessor, there's a new assessor that gets elected. But public in Cook County, like sees this story, they're outraged, it's a big scandal, the old assessor gets voted out, and a new assessor gets voted in with a mandate to fix this system. And one of the first things he did and had promised to do as part of his campaign was create a new office of data science with an open data initiative. And it's now part of a broader Cook County open data system that centralizes across many different agencies, but initially it was built up from within the assessors, the Cook County Assessors Office. And they said what we're going to do is create not just any more accurate. Algorithmic model that will be more fair because accurate but will design a new whole system in pipeline to address the problems with the Cook County assessor's office. So what I'm going to do is walk through how the assessor's office and its data science department developed this solution by reconstructing and analyzing their path through the data science life cycle that you've been learning in this class. We'll really focus on asking, you know, a question being really precise about the task before you. What exactly is it I'm trying to do? What's really involved in its stake? And it's about understanding the world that you're operating within and better understand the impacts of the model that you're building. That is you know think more about the inputs and outputs at the lifecycle. So what I'm asking you to do is situate your technical task which you can often do in abstraction how do I optimize for a kind of given loss function when I'm building my model. leaf_chunk.audio: Okay, that's the background, that's the problem. Where does the data come from? How do they intend to use it and how is it supposed to fix this problem? the rest of today's class. So the new assessor, there's a new assessor that gets elected. But public in Cook County, like sees this story, they're outraged, it's a big scandal, the old assessor gets voted out, and a new assessor gets voted in with a mandate to fix this system. And one of the first things he did and had promised to do as part of his campaign was create a new office of data science with an open data initiative. And it's now part of a broader Cook County open data system that centralizes across many different agencies, but initially it was built up from within the assessors, the Cook County Assessors Office. And they said what we're going to do is create not just any more accurate. Algorithmic model that will be more fair because accurate but will design a new whole system in pipeline to address the problems with the Cook County assessor's office. So what I'm going to do is walk through how the assessor's office and its data science department developed this solution by reconstructing and analyzing their path through the data science life cycle that you've been learning in this class. We'll really focus on asking, you know, a question being really precise about the task before you. What exactly is it I'm trying to do? What's really involved in its stake? And it's about understanding the world that you're operating within and better understand the impacts of the model that you're building. That is you know think more about the inputs and outputs at the lifecycle. So what I'm asking you to do is situate your technical task which you can often do in abstraction how do I optimize for a kind of given loss function when I'm building my model.

children.title: Data Science Lifecycle: Problem Formulation and Metrics
children.summary: The data science lifecycle outlines the workflow involved in data science, emphasizing the importance of understanding the social context of technical tasks. Key components include accurately formulating questions, such as predicting home sale prices and creating fair assessment pipelines, while also defining metrics for success.

children.title: Data Science Project: Assessing Home Values Fairly
children.summary: The project involves developing a new system for the Cook County Assessor's Office to address inaccuracies and unfairness in the old assessment system. Key objectives include accurately and impartially assessing home values, predicting sale prices within a year, and creating a robust pipeline that operates fairly across racial and income differences.

The focus is on the need for fair assessments in predicting home sale prices and creating a robust evaluation pipeline that addresses racial and income disparities.

children.title: Accuracy vs. Fairness in Property Valuation
children.summary: The segment discusses the creation of a robust pipeline for property valuation that balances accuracy and fairness. It challenges the assumption that accuracy guarantees fairness and emphasizes the need to equate statistical accuracy with fairness. Fairness is defined as the ability to assess property values while considering geographical disparities, highlighting that improving accuracy does not ensure a fair system.

children.title: Accuracy vs. Fairness in Property Valuation
children.summary: The task involves creating a robust pipeline for assessing property values that is both accurate and fair. There is a critical distinction between accuracy of prediction and fairness, with the assumption that accuracy implies fairness being challenged. The discussion aims to explore the necessary work to equate statistical accuracy with fairness in property valuation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: As they put it the task was to quote create a robust pipeline that accurately assesses property values at scale and is fair. They're essentially equating these two things which are distinct concepts. Accuracy of prediction and fairness and are trying to make one stand in for the other and trying to do the work to actually make accuracy into a way of understanding what fairness is. We may take for granted that something that's accurate is more fair but i want you to part of what i'm going to do today is try to actually denaturalize that assumption and say what work does it take to make something like statistical accuracy stand in for fairness. children.title: Defining Fairness in Property Valuation
children.summary: Fairness is defined as the ability of a pipeline to accurately assess residential property values while accounting for geographical disparities. The discussion highlights the distinction between making accurate assessments and ensuring fairness, emphasizing that improving accuracy alone does not guarantee a fair system. The focus will initially be on the technical aspect of accuracy, which is more familiar to data scientists.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So on their GitLab, they write that the Define fairness, as quote, the ability of our pipeline to accurately assess all residential property values, accounting for disparities in geography information, etc. Okay. So the task is framed as something appropriate for data scientists. If you can make the assessments more accurate, that is minimize errors in a particular way, you will definitely make for a more fair system. But there are in a sense two different problems here. Make accurate assessments, you can do that without necessarily making the system itself more fair. The fact that they're being made equivalent is important in telling. children.title: Challenges in Property Value Assessment Modeling
children.summary: The segment discusses the complexities of modeling property value assessments, focusing on the need for a consistent and fair evaluation process. Key questions include defining accurate assessments, determining success metrics, and ensuring fairness across demographics. It emphasizes that assessments are estimates of a home's value, influenced by factors such as location, materials, and market conditions, while also addressing the variability in perceptions of value among different stakeholders.

children.title: Modeling Property Value Assessment Challenges
children.summary: The focus is on building a model that accurately predicts property values, emphasizing the need for a robust pipeline that assesses home values uniformly and impartially. Key questions include defining what constitutes an accurate assessment, identifying metrics for success, and understanding the implications of fairness across different demographic groups.

children.title: Understanding Assessments: Definitions and Accuracy
children.summary: Three interrelated questions about assessments are introduced: what constitutes an assessment, what factors contribute to the accuracy of one assessment over another, and how to evaluate the accuracy of groups of assessments. An assessment is defined as an estimate of a home's value, prompting further inquiry into the determinants of that value and the variability in responses.

children.title: Understanding Home Value Assessment
children.summary: An assessment is an estimate of a home's value, which can mean different things depending on context. The discussion focuses on economic value, specifically the dollar value of a home, and raises questions about what determines this value.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: An assessment obviously is an estimate of the value of a home we've been talking about that for a while, duh. What do we mean by value here? We're estimating the value of a home. The word value means a lot of different things. You know, every day like we talk about all kinds of different values. There's many ways of valuing a home thinking about its use value. It's usefulness to somebody. It's value with something that I desire or like or want. Um, obviously we're not talking about moral values like trust, whether you're dependable, or a physical measurement that has different values. But of course we're talking about an economic value. children.title: Assessing Economic Value of Homes
children.summary: Assessment is defined as an estimate of a home's value, which can be influenced by various factors such as construction materials and property size. The concept of fairness in pricing can also be derived from external notions, prompting questions about what determines a home's value and how it is established.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's not just some intrinsic property of this physical structure sitting there. And there's many different ways you can actually talk about determining the economic value of a home even. You could refer it to its construction materials and property size. children.title: Assessing Home Value: Perspectives and Questions
children.summary: Assessment is defined as an estimate of a home's value, influenced by various factors such as location, materials, quality, and size. The segment raises questions about what determines a home's value and how individuals, including real estate experts and homeowners, perceive this value.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It could be what a real estate expert thinks based on some combination of location, materials, quality or size. It could also just be what a homeowner thinks. What is my property worth? children.title: Determining Economic Price of Homes
children.summary: The economic price of a home is determined by its market value, which reflects the price someone actually paid at the time of sale. An assessment is an estimate of a home's value, but the true value is only established at the moment of sale, particularly for property tax assessments.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But I think you all probably already assume that none of these things actually go into determining what is that you know how do you actually determine economic price of a home. That thing that you need to actually use is a target variable if you want to predict homes that haven't been sold in a long time. In this case, what do you think they use? They use market value. children.title: Market Value of Homes: Determination and Assessment
children.summary: Market value is defined as the price determined by the interaction of buyers and sellers at the moment of a sale. This value is assessed based on various factors, with the 'true value' only being established during the sale, particularly relevant for property tax assessments.

children.title: Home Value Determinants and Market Dynamics
children.summary: The value of a home is influenced by a complex network of social interactions, perceptions, and financial instruments, making it a fluid data point rather than a stable truth. Market value is determined at the moment of sale, reflecting the willingness of buyers to pay, and while it may seem arbitrary, there are underlying reasons for price stability and patterned changes over time. When a home is unsold, its market value remains indeterminate, highlighting the complexities of valuation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Rather, the value of a home is a product of a social interaction that takes place at one moment in time that's structured by a whole network of perceptions and information, beliefs, desire, convenience, Existing financial instruments, taste, class, perceptions of class about the neighborhood, perceptions of the race of the neighborhood, you could go on, all of these things can structure exactly why a house sells for what it does at a particular moment in time. It's a very ephemeral data point that's taken to be something that's a hard ground truth that's very stable. But a house could have, you know, if that one person didn't show up who loved this house for some reason, it could be sold again the next day for a different price. So long as there's just someone willing to pay that price for sell for that price. So it's not stable the value that you're using as a target variable or a ground truth, but it's also not arbitrary. There's good reason there's a logic behind why things usually get sold for roughly what they get sold for. reasons why prices tend to be relatively stable across, you know, short periods of time, and when they change, they change in patterned ways. So when a home is not sold. Its market value is not just in uncertain, it's not that we don't know what the market value is when it hasn't been sold.
