children.title: Home Value Assessment and Market Indeterminacy
children.summary: Home value is assessed as an estimate based on market conditions, with its 'true value' only realized at the point of sale. The concept of value is indeterminate, reliant on social legitimization and institutional assessments, which vary based on different needs, such as mortgage evaluations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's not uncertain the way that you might be uncertain about where is a star based on, you know, I have all these different astronomical observations and I'm uncertain where it actually is. And I have to use, you know, averages to try and figure out what I think, you know, the best position of it is. It's not uncertain in that way. It's more deeply it's indeterminate because it has no fixed value outside of our socially legitimated acts of valuing it in a marketplace. That is, it's given value by being sold. And in between, it's not value lists, but it's value again is indeterminate. Its value is what different institutions that need to know what its value is decide that it's worth. You know, what you want to leverage it to take a mortgage out on it, you need to determine what its value is. children.title: Property Assessment: Market Value Determination
children.summary: Property assessment involves estimating a home's market value to determine its tax burden. The true value is only realized at the moment of sale, making it impractical for assessors to sell properties regularly. Statistical tools are used to generate reliable estimates, allowing assessors to ensure fair tax contributions from property owners.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So again, there's social institutions that need to decide, and in some ways make determinate something that is at root indeterminate. So, um, if you're trying to determine the price of a home, it's market value in order to assess its tax burden. The only way of reliably was say, 100% certainty of knowing its value. You can say this with perfect accuracy is to actually sell it. In a sense, an estimate is only really truly accurate if you test it by selling the very same property the next day. And even then it's only going to be accurate for a short period of time because the market might fluctuate. It might be worth something different. Unfortunately, if you're a property assessor, selling every property every three years isn't really a great way to make an assessment. That's why we use statistical tools to do it. You need to have a pretty good idea of what a property is worth if you want its owners to pay their fair share of taxes on it. Video: 1: 1. Question/Problem Formulation

children.title: Estimating Property Values with Data Science
children.summary: Reliable property value estimates are difficult due to infrequent sales and outdated data. Data scientists can predict property values by analyzing home features and recent sales prices, similar to the methods used by platforms like Zillow and Redfin, which generate estimates based on historical data.

children.title: Estimating Property Values with Data Science
children.summary: Reliable property value estimates are challenging due to infrequent sales and outdated data. Data scientists can predict property values using features of homes and recent sales prices, similar to how platforms like Zillow and Redfin operate. These platforms generate estimates based on past data, predicting potential selling prices.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You can't make everyone sell their property every few years to truly know its market value. And since so many properties haven't been sold for years, for decades, every year that passes makes that previous sale even less reliable as an indicator. So you need to make estimates. So you're all data scientists or interested in data science or want to learn how to do it in some way. So you have some sense of where this is going. You're probably thinking, well, with the right data about lots of different homes, their features, their characteristics, and with enough some subset of recent sales prices, you can probably predict the value of a property pretty reliably. Even if you're not a data scientist, you probably know about websites like Zillow and Redfin that do this and are constantly updating their estimates or property values in a similar way. They of course don't know the actual value what it will definitely sell for, but they make estimates they make predictions based on past data. They predict what it would sell for if it sold in the next week or perhaps in the next year. How do you think they do this? Let's look at this with a little more precisely by starting with the data. leaf_chunk.audio: You can't make everyone sell their property every few years to truly know its market value. And since so many properties haven't been sold for years, for decades, every year that passes makes that previous sale even less reliable as an indicator. So you need to make estimates. So you're all data scientists or interested in data science or want to learn how to do it in some way. So you have some sense of where this is going. You're probably thinking, well, with the right data about lots of different homes, their features, their characteristics, and with enough some subset of recent sales prices, you can probably predict the value of a property pretty reliably. Even if you're not a data scientist, you probably know about websites like Zillow and Redfin that do this and are constantly updating their estimates or property values in a similar way. They of course don't know the actual value what it will definitely sell for, but they make estimates they make predictions based on past data. They predict what it would sell for if it sold in the next week or perhaps in the next year. How do you think they do this? Let's look at this with a little more precisely by starting with the data.

children.title: Data Acquisition and Home Value Estimation
children.summary: The complexities of data acquisition for property assessments are discussed, focusing on available data for the Cook County Assessor's Office and the characteristics of properties in Projects A1 and A2. Homeowners estimate property values by comparing recent sales of similar homes, influenced by size and features, though these estimates can be unreliable. County assessors analyze property sales using advanced statistical techniques, ensuring accurate modeling by filtering out atypical transactions. Linear models are created to predict sales prices based on property characteristics, emphasizing the importance of data quality and completeness, while data infrastructure and exploratory data analysis (EDA) are essential for identifying predictive features.

children.title: Data Acquisition: Assessing Property Characteristics
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the complexities of data acquisition in the context of property assessments, particularly for the Cook County Assessor's Office. Key questions include what data is available, what is needed, and how to sample additional data. The data sets for Project A1 and A2 include various property characteristics such as age, central air conditioning, and number of bedrooms.

Audio: Because that's the next step in the life cycle right now we've just been talking about framing the problem. What data do we have? What do we need? How will we sample more data, et cetera? So in the case of the Cook County Assessor's Office, you've got two data sets and you've got these data that you're going to be working with these data sets in Project A1 and A2. You have property characteristics on the one hand, so for every single property, every single residential property in Cook County, you have a big list of different property characteristics, and you see some of them listed here, the age of the property, central air conditioning apartments, attic finish, attic type bedrooms, in your number of bedrooms, building square feet, does it have a basement, et cetera? children.title: Estimating Home Value Using Sales Data
children.summary: Homeowners often estimate their property's value by comparing it to recent sales of similar homes. Factors such as size and additional features influence these estimates, which can be rough and unreliable due to small sample sizes. The discussion also includes various data points collected about properties, including sales data and physical characteristics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The list goes on and on and on. There's a lot of different data points you can collect about any individual property. And on the other, you have sales data, all homes in the county, and then you select only the most recent sales data because the longer ago it is, the less useful it is for you. You don't have to be a data scientist, of course, to think this way. Everyday homeowners do this all the time. You live on a street save identical homes, your neighbor sells their house for $500,000. Okay, it's pretty safe bet that your house would also sell for $500,000 if you sold it in the near future. But what if your house is a little bit larger than your neighbors? Say an extra 200 square feet? Maybe you see another house down the street that's a little larger than yours, and it has an extra bathroom and it sells for $700,000. How do you decide where the value of your house lies in between them? You could say it's $600,000, somewhere in the middle. And that's a really rough estimate, not very reliable, and you're obviously working up a very small sample size. Video: 1: Feature Name

leaf_chunk.audio: The list goes on and on and on. There's a lot of different data points you can collect about any individual property. And on the other, you have sales data, all homes in the county, and then you select only the most recent sales data because the longer ago it is, the less useful it is for you. You don't have to be a data scientist, of course, to think this way. Everyday homeowners do this all the time. You live on a street save identical homes, your neighbor sells their house for $500,000. Okay, it's pretty safe bet that your house would also sell for $500,000 if you sold it in the near future. But what if your house is a little bit larger than your neighbors? Say an extra 200 square feet? Maybe you see another house down the street that's a little larger than yours, and it has an extra bathroom and it sells for $700,000. How do you decide where the value of your house lies in between them? You could say it's $600,000, somewhere in the middle. And that's a really rough estimate, not very reliable, and you're obviously working up a very small sample size.

children.title: Data Analysis for County Assessors
children.summary: County assessors utilize extensive data and advanced statistical techniques to analyze property sales. They focus on arms-length transactions, filtering out atypical sales, such as those sold at discounted prices to family members, to ensure accurate modeling. The data includes sales records from 2013-2019 and various property characteristics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But the county assessors in the CCAO have a lot more data, and they have sophisticated statistical techniques, like all of you were learning. So looking at square feet in bathrooms of every home, sales data on every arms-length transaction. Arms length, by the way, here means that it was It wasn't being sold on a big discount to a family member. So some properties are going to be sold for weirdly small number amount of money because it's like a parent's like selling the property to their child for a poultry sum. So you have to actually filter figure out where those are and filter them now and not use those because they're going to mess up your model because they're not arms length market prices. leaf_chunk.audio: But the county assessors in the CCAO have a lot more data, and they have sophisticated statistical techniques, like all of you were learning. So looking at square feet in bathrooms of every home, sales data on every arms-length transaction. Arms length, by the way, here means that it was It wasn't being sold on a big discount to a family member. So some properties are going to be sold for weirdly small number amount of money because it's like a parent's like selling the property to their child for a poultry sum. So you have to actually filter figure out where those are and filter them now and not use those because they're going to mess up your model because they're not arms length market prices.

children.title: Creating Linear Models for Property Value Prediction
children.summary: A linear model can predict sales prices by analyzing patterns in datasets with known sale prices and property characteristics. Key considerations include the quality, completeness, and consistency of the data, as well as missing attributes that could significantly impact property values, such as proximity to airports or floodplains. Understanding how the data was collected and its reliability is crucial for accurate predictions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So all this data makes it possible to create a linear model to predict sales price, the fair market value of unsold properties, by basically uncovering patterns and data sets containing known sale prices and characteristics of similar and nearby properties, training a model on this data and applying it to all the properties about which you don't have sales data. And so one of the big things you have to think about is like, what is in my data? How good is my data? How complete is my data? How consistent is it? How might the attributes be differentially reported? Which data is missing and for which neighborhoods is the data going to be spotter for some neighborhoods rather than others? And they even went out and realized that there were important predictors that they didn't have reliable data on, such as, are the homes in a floodplain? How closer they to an airport? That is, if you're in an area that, you know, near mid-way or old-hair airports and planes are landing over you all the time, that might be a big reason for why your property values are lower than they might otherwise be. And you have to figure out how do you track these improvements? How do you update them? How do you figure out how to get sufficient data? Video: 1: Feature Name

leaf_chunk.audio: So all this data makes it possible to create a linear model to predict sales price, the fair market value of unsold properties, by basically uncovering patterns and data sets containing known sale prices and characteristics of similar and nearby properties, training a model on this data and applying it to all the properties about which you don't have sales data. And so one of the big things you have to think about is like, what is in my data? How good is my data? How complete is my data? How consistent is it? How might the attributes be differentially reported? Which data is missing and for which neighborhoods is the data going to be spotter for some neighborhoods rather than others? And they even went out and realized that there were important predictors that they didn't have reliable data on, such as, are the homes in a floodplain? How closer they to an airport? That is, if you're in an area that, you know, near mid-way or old-hair airports and planes are landing over you all the time, that might be a big reason for why your property values are lower than they might otherwise be. And you have to figure out how do you track these improvements? How do you update them? How do you figure out how to get sufficient data?

children.title: Building Data Infrastructure and EDA for Predictive Features
children.summary: Data scientists must establish infrastructure for collecting data and perform exploratory data analysis (EDA) to identify important features predictive of sales price. The predictive value of features can vary by neighborhood, as combinations of attributes, such as the presence of an extra bedroom alongside other characteristics, influence their contribution to value. Additionally, considerations include the reporting of attributes, missing data across neighborhoods, and the potential for creating new attributes from existing data.

leaf_chunk.text:

Which sets of attributes are most predictive of sales price? They note that this that which features our most predictive actually changes neighborhood to neighborhood. children.title: Predictive Attributes and Data Science Life Cycle
children.summary: Location significantly influences property value variation across neighborhoods, necessitating different models for analysis. Misleading data points, such as non-arms-length transactions, and unreliable data in low-income neighborhoods highlight the need for better data collection. The data science life cycle is iterative, focusing on data quality, completeness, model predictions, and ensuring fairness in model accuracy.

children.title: Data Analysis: Predictive Attributes and Distribution
children.summary: Different models may be needed for various neighborhoods due to location being the largest driver of county-wide property value variation, influencing how other features are weighted. The analysis also highlights potential misleading data points, such as non-arms-length transactions, and notes that low-income neighborhoods often have less reliable data, necessitating improved data collection practices and new sources.

children.title: Data Science Life Cycle: Understanding and Modeling Data
children.summary: The data science life cycle is iterative, often requiring revisiting earlier stages to address issues with data quality or completeness. It involves assessing the data to determine if it is sufficient for modeling, making predictions, and evaluating model accuracy. Additionally, the importance of ensuring fairness in the model's accuracy is emphasized.

children.title: Model Accuracy, Fairness, and Social Trust in Valuation
children.summary: Model accuracy in housing price prediction is evaluated using RMSE and holdout data to prevent overfitting, though it has limitations. Metrics like the coefficient of dispersion highlight fairness issues in data science, influenced by expert decisions and societal values. In taxation, acceptable error levels may prioritize fairness for lower income groups, affecting model development choices. Social trust impacts property valuation accuracy, necessitating transparency and community engagement to ensure assessments reflect relational values.

children.title: Model Accuracy and RMSE in Housing Price Prediction
children.summary: Model accuracy in predicting housing prices is evaluated using holdout data to avoid overfitting. The process involves identifying patterns in datasets of known sales and requires quantitative measures for confidence. Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) is a primary metric for assessing model accuracy, although it has limitations regarding error distributions and valuation biases.

children.title: Model Accuracy and RMSE in Housing Price Prediction
children.summary: Model accuracy is assessed using holdout data to predict property sales prices, ensuring the model is not overfitting. Predicting housing prices involves identifying patterns in datasets of known sales and requires quantitative measures for confidence in predictions. Various methods evaluate model accuracy, with Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) being a key metric, though it has limitations in revealing error distributions and specific valuation biases.

children.title: Model Accuracy: Testing with Holdout Data
children.summary: To assess the accuracy of a model, use a test data set that the model was not trained on, predicting the sales price of properties. By comparing the model's predictions to actual sales prices, one can evaluate its performance and ensure the model is not overfitting, which is a key consideration for project work.

children.title: Predicting Housing Prices: Metrics and Confidence
children.summary: The process involves predicting the sale price of unsold properties by identifying patterns in datasets of known sale prices and property characteristics. A quantitative measure of prediction accuracy is essential, providing confidence that predictions for individual houses will align closely with recent sales data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is precisely what you're going to do and precisely how you're going to kind of then you're going to make the predictions and you're going to try to evaluate it according to certain metrics. How close was I was I close enough? And then you go back and say I can accurately predict the price of any one individual house that has not been sold recently because at scale my model produces values that are pretty close to the recent sales data about other houses in most cases. So you need some quantitative measure of this closeness. And when you have this, it gives you at least as the data scientist some confidence that any one prediction about any individual house, even though you don't know for certain, it gives you some confidence that you can transpose, you can translate from the statistical aggregate to any one house and say, I'm pretty sure that that one's going to be close to, if only it were to sell on the next year and I could verify, in fact, if I was that close. children.title: Evaluating Model Accuracy: RMSE and Limitations
children.summary: The evaluation of model accuracy will involve calculating the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), which is a crucial tool in assessing model performance. However, RMSE has limitations as it does not provide insights into the distribution of errors or their specific occurrences, such as whether errors indicate overvaluation or undervaluation in different segments of data.

children.title: Limitations of RMSE in Property Appraisal
children.summary: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) provides a rough measure of aggregate error but has limitations in evaluating fairness in property appraisal. It does not capture the distribution or sign of errors, nor does it indicate the regressivity of the model. Data scientists can rely on professional standards set by the International Association of Assessment Officers to guide their evaluation of fairness.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It just gives you a rough measure of the overall aggregate error. That might actually not be good enough to ensure that your system's not regressive. If the error that does exist over values less expensive homes and over values more expensive homes. You don't actually see that when you're looking at RMSC. Broad metrics of accuracy like RMSE does have limits for evaluating fairness in property appraisal because it doesn't tell you the distribution of the errors, the sign of the errors that is are the positive or negative, and it doesn't tell you anything about the relative size of the errors. Broad metrics of accuracy like RMSE does have limits for evaluating fairness in property appraisal because it doesn't tell you the distribution of the errors, the sign of the errors that is are the positive or negative, and it doesn't tell you anything about the relative size of the errors. In short, It doesn't tell you anything about the regressivity of your model which is precisely what you're trying to worry about so our ms is you know i can't get the thing you reach for simple you're gonna learn to use it here in this. in this project, but you also need to recognize what exactly as a measure it doesn't capture. So what's the alternative that? In some ways the world of expertise. That there are experts out there working this has made it easy for you the data scientist you don't have to reinvent the wheel. You don't have to make hard choices about RMSC or figure out what's another metric that's going to be better and which ones the right one. You don't have to make hard choices about the meaning of fairness it seems because as a data scientist working for this particular institution there's professional standards their rules set out by the international association of assessment officers.

children.title: Standards and Fairness in Data Science
children.summary: Data scientists utilize metrics like the coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential to assess model accuracy, which are shaped by expert decisions and reflect the values of those in power. Additionally, the social construction of expertise raises questions about the fairness of these metrics and the acceptable error levels, particularly in fields such as real estate.

children.title: Standards in Data Science: Political Implications
children.summary: Data scientists use established metrics such as coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential to evaluate model accuracy, often without questioning their implications. These standards, while essential, are influenced by expert decisions and reflect the values and thresholds set by those in power, highlighting the importance of understanding their limitations and motivations.

children.title: Standards in Data Science: Political Implications
children.summary: Data scientists often rely on established metrics like coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential to assess model accuracy without questioning their implications. While these standards are essential for practical work, they are shaped by expert decisions and carry political undertones, as they are not neutral but rather reflect certain values and thresholds determined by those in power. Understanding the limitations and motivations behind these standards is crucial for data scientists.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: that you just follow and then you don't have to decide which ones to use. You just say, do I actually fall within these metrics that you might remember this slide from earlier? Things like coefficient of dispersion, price-related differential, which are just two different ways of trying to measure things like how is the error being distributed and where? So the associations create standardized tests and determine the appropriate thresholds for what counts as close for model estimating property values at scale. It says these are the best practices when you're working for this institution. And for a lot of data scientists you simply go in and say okay, that's the experts say that's a sort of determination by assessment officers. I'm just good with data so I'm going to do build the best models I can to meet their definitions but I'm not actually going to think anymore about what does this mean and how does it translate into actual effects on people in the world. So most data scientists might just accept these professional standards. And maybe again that's for the best it's inevitable. You're not going to reinvent the wheel every time you do a project and rethink every little thing from the ground up. There's limits to what you can do. for time and resources. But you should be cognizant that any standards like these were made for reasons. and know what their possible limitations might be. They're not natural facts about the world, about fairness in the world. They're the result of decisions made by experts. And not only are experts not infallible, their forms of knowledge making are always political. They may present themselves as neutral. This is just the kind of standard if you fall within this, then you are technically and objectively in the clear. It's actually a political determination to say, this is an acceptable threshold, and this is the kind of threshold, or this is the direction that your values have to go in. Their forms of knowledge making are always political. Don't forget that it was the professionalizing real estate assessors in the 1920s and 30s who encoded racist, risky and real estate practices precisely in the name of creating objective, expert knowledge practices. leaf_chunk.audio: that you just follow and then you don't have to decide which ones to use. You just say, do I actually fall within these metrics that you might remember this slide from earlier? Things like coefficient of dispersion, price-related differential, which are just two different ways of trying to measure things like how is the error being distributed and where? So the associations create standardized tests and determine the appropriate thresholds for what counts as close for model estimating property values at scale. It says these are the best practices when you're working for this institution. And for a lot of data scientists you simply go in and say okay, that's the experts say that's a sort of determination by assessment officers. I'm just good with data so I'm going to do build the best models I can to meet their definitions but I'm not actually going to think anymore about what does this mean and how does it translate into actual effects on people in the world. So most data scientists might just accept these professional standards. And maybe again that's for the best it's inevitable. You're not going to reinvent the wheel every time you do a project and rethink every little thing from the ground up. There's limits to what you can do. for time and resources. But you should be cognizant that any standards like these were made for reasons. and know what their possible limitations might be. They're not natural facts about the world, about fairness in the world. They're the result of decisions made by experts. And not only are experts not infallible, their forms of knowledge making are always political. They may present themselves as neutral. This is just the kind of standard if you fall within this, then you are technically and objectively in the clear. It's actually a political determination to say, this is an acceptable threshold, and this is the kind of threshold, or this is the direction that your values have to go in. Their forms of knowledge making are always political. Don't forget that it was the professionalizing real estate assessors in the 1920s and 30s who encoded racist, risky and real estate practices precisely in the name of creating objective, expert knowledge practices.

children.title: Social Construction of Expertise and Metrics of Fairness
children.summary: Expertise and standards, including statistical significance thresholds like P values, are socially constructed and subject to reconsideration. In real estate, metrics such as the coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential are influenced by expert decisions, raising questions about their fairness and the acceptable level of errors in these metrics.

children.title: Social Construction of Expertise and Standards
children.summary: Expertise and standards are socially constructed and should not be taken for granted as inherent truths. The thresholds used in statistical significance, like P values, are products of expert consensus and can be reconsidered. Additionally, metrics related to real estate and racial inequality illustrate how expert decisions shape perceptions of fairness, rather than reflecting natural facts.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm not trying to say expertise is bad, I'm trying to say it socially constructed and complicated, and you want to be careful around what happens when you black box these standards and take them for granted, and see them as just part of the way the world is rather than as the product of specific choices that are political and value-laden. After all, like the P values you learn about in statistical significance testing, these thresholds are in a sense made up, and then they're black boxed for most users. It doesn't mean there are betrayary exactly, they're the product of expert agreement, a threshold that was stabilized by some kind of local consensus at a certain moment in time, but they could be otherwise. And there might be good reasons to make them differently. Video: 1: Real estate and racial inequality in the United States

leaf_chunk.audio: I'm not trying to say expertise is bad, I'm trying to say it socially constructed and complicated, and you want to be careful around what happens when you black box these standards and take them for granted, and see them as just part of the way the world is rather than as the product of specific choices that are political and value-laden. After all, like the P values you learn about in statistical significance testing, these thresholds are in a sense made up, and then they're black boxed for most users. It doesn't mean there are betrayary exactly, they're the product of expert agreement, a threshold that was stabilized by some kind of local consensus at a certain moment in time, but they could be otherwise. And there might be good reasons to make them differently.

children.title: Metrics of Accuracy and Fairness in Real Estate
children.summary: Discussion centers on the fairness of standardized metrics used in real estate, highlighting that metrics like the coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential are influenced by expert decisions rather than natural fairness. It raises questions about the permissibility of errors in these metrics and suggests that certain intuitions guide expectations of fairness in the distribution of errors.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Because there might be reasons that you think. Um. Are they necessarily the most fair metrics of accuracy? and are there alternatives? Maybe so, maybe not. Just to give you one example of how this could be different, you could determine that any errors are only permissible when they're progressive. The coefficient of dispersion and price-related differential do things like measure. How just how regressive can you be up to a certain point it permits a certain amount of regressiveness in the distribution of errors. But that's not natural. That's not like the way things have to be. It's about certain intuitions about what it's fair to expect. Video: 1: Real estate and racial inequality in the United States

children.title: Model Accuracy and Fairness in Taxation
children.summary: Acceptable errors in taxation models may prioritize fairness for lower income quartiles over overall accuracy, influenced by political choices. The choice of evaluation metrics, such as RMSE versus regressivity, significantly affects model development, with a noted preference for minimizing regressivity despite high error rates in expensive properties. A poll on model fairness led to discussions about taxing higher-valued homes and the complexities of defining accuracy in property assessments, emphasizing the need for a broader market context.

children.title: Model Accuracy, Selection, and Evaluation in Taxation
children.summary: Acceptable errors in models may prioritize reducing tax burdens for lower quartiles over overall accuracy, reflecting political choices about fairness. The choice of metric significantly impacts model development, with trade-offs between minimizing RMSE and regressivity. Evaluation of modeling options shows a preference for minimizing regressivity, despite high error rates in expensive properties due to infrequent sales.

children.title: Model Accuracy and Political Decisions in Taxation
children.summary: Acceptable errors in models may prioritize reducing tax burdens for lower quartiles over overall accuracy, as indicated by RMSE. The IAAO allows for a degree of regressivity in property taxes, reflecting political choices regarding fairness and accuracy standards. The framing of acceptable regressivity is a political decision, emphasizing that fairness cannot be captured by a single metric.

children.title: Model Selection: Metrics and Fairness Trade-offs
children.summary: The choice of metric significantly impacts the model or pipeline developed, as different models minimize various forms of error. Key considerations include balancing RMSE and regressivity, with options like minimizing RMSE despite regressivity or prioritizing lower regressivity at the cost of higher RMSE. The framing of these choices reflects political decisions about acceptable error distributions, particularly regarding tax burdens on different property values.

leaf_chunk.text:

Is it a model that minimizes RMSC? It's just RMSC is as low as it could be, but it's still somewhat regressive. Is it a model that minimizes regressivity but has a higher RMSC? Is it a model that minimizes regressivity, but its highest error rates are clustered around the most expensive properties? children.title: Model Evaluation: Error Rates and Fairness
children.summary: The discussion centers on the evaluation of different modeling options regarding fairness and error rates. Option three, which minimizes regressivity but has high error rates clustered around expensive properties, was the most popular choice among participants. The high error rates in expensive properties are attributed to their infrequent sales, resulting in fewer data points.

children.title: Fairness Models and Property Assessment Accuracy
children.summary: Participants engaged in a poll on model fairness, weighing overall error (RMSE) against regressivity, and discussed their voting preferences. The conversation explored the implications of taxing higher-valued homes and the political views on wealth distribution. Additionally, the complexities of defining accuracy in property assessments were examined, highlighting that accuracy is relative to the broader housing market and collective data rather than isolated evaluations.

children.title: Active Poll: Fairness and Error Models
children.summary: Participants engaged in an active poll regarding their intuitions about model fairness, specifically considering options that balance overall error (RMSE) and regressivity. The discussion included reasons for voting preferences, focusing on the trade-offs between lower error and progressive versus regressive models.

children.title: Taxation Fairness and Model Evaluation Polls
children.summary: Discussion centers on the fairness of taxing higher-valued homes at higher rates, with an emphasis on political perspectives regarding wealth distribution. An active poll engages participants in evaluating models of fairness based on overall error (RMSE) and regressivity, presenting various scenarios for consideration.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's more fair to tax the higher valued homes at higher rates. Well, and why do you think that? Yeah, I mean, there's a sense of it's your sense of political it's it's your political orientation to say that this is more fair. Like they're wealthy they can pay more it's okay if you're going to mess up somewhere it should be on the people who actually can bear that burden right more than the people who who can't. Yeah, I think that's a really valuable assess like evaluation. By the way, that's kind of closer to where I would land too. It doesn't mean that you all have to agree with the two of us. children.title: Understanding Accuracy in Property Assessments
children.summary: The discussion highlights the complexities of defining accuracy in property assessments, emphasizing that an individual assessment's accuracy is relative to its relationship with other properties and the broader housing market. The accuracy of a single property is judged based on the model's overall performance compared to all other properties, particularly those that have been sold, indicating that perceived accuracy is contingent upon collective data rather than isolated evaluations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That clearly is not the default answer from all of you, although I'm kind of pleased that you've moved from A to C is the dominant answer. But I think there's a kind of a deeper politics, a kind of more equalizing and progressive politics that is suggested by D, but I know it probably makes you uncomfortable as a data scientist to imagine I'm going to build something that's like, gets it wrong in some technical sense. children.title: Social Trust and Fairness in Property Valuation
children.summary: The accuracy of property assessments is influenced by social trust, affecting perceptions of property values and requiring socio-technical performance. Assessment accuracy relies on transparency in statistical practices and the interplay between social context and trust, with implications for wealth distribution and fairness, particularly between affluent and less wealthy neighborhoods. Engagement with affected communities is essential to ensure assessments reflect relational values and address the broader justice aspects beyond mere accuracy.

children.title: Social Trust and Fairness in Property Valuation
children.summary: The accuracy of property assessments is influenced by social trust, impacting perceptions of property values. Home value assessment requires socio-technical performance, emphasizing trust to connect individual properties with market trends while ensuring legitimacy. Modeling fairness involves creating accurate numbers that also promote social fairness, with complexities arising from hidden variables affecting model performance.

children.title: Social Trust and Property Value Accuracy
children.summary: The accuracy of property assessments is influenced by social trust, affecting perceptions of individual property values. An active poll is conducted to gauge intuitions about mean fairness in models, presenting options that weigh overall error against regressivity in property valuation.

children.title: Modeling Fairness: Socio-Technical Considerations
children.summary: Building a model involves creating numbers that not only reflect accuracy but also serve as persuasive tools for social fairness. The relationship between technical accuracy and social fairness is complex, as discrepancies can arise from hidden variables not captured in the data. A poll is conducted to gauge intuitions about mean fairness in model performance, presenting various scenarios regarding error rates and regressivity.

children.title: Assessment Accuracy: Factors and Transparency
children.summary: Assessment is defined in evaluating home sale prices, focusing on the reliability of statistical models and the factors contributing to assessment accuracy. Key discussions include the importance of transparency in statistical practices for public auditing, the social robustness of statistical inferences, and the interplay between social context and trust in statistical arguments. Additionally, the effectiveness of statistical objectivity in assessments hinges on public acceptance and trust, emphasizing that fair algorithms require a deeper understanding beyond mere optimization.

children.title: Transparency in Statistical Practices
children.summary: Making the statistical pipeline transparent allows public auditing and contestation of practices, highlighting potential omissions or inaccuracies. This openness is essential for maintaining the credibility of statistical predictions, even as it opens the door for criticism.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We make this pipeline transparent, we show it to the public so anybody can come in and audit and see what are we doing? How are we actually, you know, we're showing our work, we're allowing people to come in and contest our practice and say, oh, here's some important things that you've left out. But there's always the possibility of course it's something doesn't add up, which means that there's ways for people to contest it and ultimately try to discredit the entire practice that you're trying to put together. And that's part of the point of what I'm saying. I'm not saying this to undermine the power of statistics and its predictions. Video: 1: 1. Question/Problem Formulation

children.title: Social Robustness of Statistical Inferences
children.summary: Making socially robust inferences requires understanding the social context and meanings of numbers, including how they are interpreted and their impact on beliefs and trust. Trust plays a crucial role in when we accept statistical arguments. Additionally, the value of an assessment is relational, arising from the interaction of social and technical elements within a sociotechnical system.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Quite the opposite. I'm saying that making socially robust inferences and predictions about the world. Make them actually robust socially involves understanding the social work that those numbers do in the world, how they're interpreted, how they're read, how they move through the world and actually shift things, how they instill, how they relate to beliefs and values and social trust, that are needed to help those numbers do the work. They don't magically do it on their own. Numbers. Do work because they have social meanings and social existence and that means they don't always mean the same thing to everyone. Trust is at the heart of all of us. Why, when do we trust a numbers in statistical arguments? Or not? And this is why people still go to the review board. Video: 1: 1. Question/Problem Formulation

children.title: Statistical Objectivity and Assessment Accuracy
children.summary: The effectiveness of statistical objectivity in assessments relies on the willingness of people to accept and trust the numbers produced. Simply achieving desired metrics and transparency does not guarantee fair outcomes, as the interplay of social and technical elements in a sociotechnical system influences the accuracy of assessments.

leaf_chunk.text:

And try to say don't trust those numbers, don't trust these practices. and continue to hire tax lawyers to go and try to undermine the system. So in the end with the people creating the system relying on was if we make such a good and open system it'll be so hard for these people to actually go and contest it because we're gonna have the power of you know a numerical statistical objectivity on our side. But statistical objectivity is only as good as the people who are willing to listen to it and buy into it. And so whether it works or not depends on more than simply. children.title: Fair Algorithms and Social Trust in Assessments
children.summary: Creating fair algorithms involves more than just optimizing for a loss function; it requires understanding the impact of outputs on society. Even with well-optimized models, the effectiveness in real-world applications, such as tax assessments, depends on social trust and the context in which these outputs are presented. The relationship between social and technical elements is crucial in determining the accuracy and acceptance of assessments.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There's a gap there too that you don't want to take for granted. Creating fair algorithms is not just about an abstract formal relationship between the inputs and outputs of your model. It's about what those outputs do and how they do them as well. So for the sake of argument. This is for the sake of argument here. Um. Let's pretend that your model is perfectly optimized for a particular loss function that satisfies our intuitions about what fairness should mean. There's no guarantee that the printing of those numbers and their sent being sent out to people's homes and then being taken to tax review boards are actually going to result in people paying what some others did to be their fair share in taxes. It may be an important step. An important ingredient. But it's not everything. And even if it does work, it's not simply because the numbers are irresistibly compelling, but because they drew upon this reservoir of social trust, they were part of powerful social arguments about doing those work. Doing that kind of work and if that trust has been diminished, it may be hard to win it back, through appealing to yet more numbers and yet more other forms of expertise. Video: 1: 1. children.title: Challenges in Fair Property Assessments
children.summary: The CCAO faces difficulties in creating fair property assessments and building social trust, as highlighted by news stories of rising residential taxes due to decreasing commercial taxes from under-evaluations. Large corporations effectively use tax lawyers to lower their rates, impacting homeowners negatively. The complexity of property value assessments is emphasized, noting that a home's value is relational and shaped by sociotechnical interactions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And so that's why despite all their best efforts, the CCAO is still struggling to create fair assessments and engender social trust as witnessed by some of these news stories that have come out over the last few years. Stories like the one on the right. Total taxes for residential properties went up because commercial taxes went down due to continuing under evaluations. Actually, point to the fact that tax review board hasn't been disrupted because the people most effectively leveraging tax lawyers are large corporations owning large commercial properties that have continued to be successful in lowering their overall rates, which necessarily increases rates on private homeowners. But it's more complicated if you look at this distribution on the right and you know anything about Chicago and its maps. Video: 1: 1. Question/Problem Formulation

children.title: Impact of Assessments on Wealth Distribution and Fairness
children.summary: Wealthy neighborhoods saw significant increases in assessments, while less wealthy, predominantly black neighborhoods experienced decreases, indicating a complex redistribution dynamic that raises fairness concerns and potential backlash from stakeholders. Fairness in assessments requires accuracy but also involves broader political implications, as the consequences of assessment errors differ greatly between socioeconomic groups. The social effects of assessment accuracy must be considered, as errors can have severe impacts on individuals, necessitating engagement with affected communities to foster trust and ensure that assessments reflect relational values.

children.title: Impact of Assessments on Wealth Distribution
children.summary: Wealthy neighborhoods experienced significant increases in assessments, while less wealthy, predominantly black neighborhoods in South Chicago saw decreases, suggesting a complex redistribution dynamic. This raises questions about fairness and the potential backlash from powerful stakeholders, undermining trust in the system. Additionally, the concept of assessment is explored, emphasizing its relational value as a product of social and technical interactions.

children.title: Fairness vs. Accuracy in Assessments
children.summary: A fair system requires demonstrable accuracy, but fairness encompasses more than just input-output relationships. Questions of fairness often extend beyond a data scientist's role, involving political considerations such as the implications of tax rates. The impact of over or under-assessment varies significantly between socioeconomic groups, highlighting that lower-income individuals may face catastrophic consequences compared to wealthier homeowners who experience it as a minor inconvenience.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: As I think we've had the big kind of takeaway that we've been demonstrating throughout entirety of today's lecture. A fair system almost certainly needs to be accurate in some demonstrable sense, but it's not necessarily fair. Fairness is about more than some way of quantifying the relationship between inputs and outputs of the system. All kinds of questions about fairness show up here too that might feel like they go beyond your responsibility as a data scientist. Is a neutral or flat tax rate really fair? Is it about a matter of policy rather than about how close your model reflects the policy? That's often not part of the decision space, the data scientist, but it is a larger political question that matters for the work that you do. Other levels of fairness are often occluded by hidden sort of made harder to see by excessively focusing on accuracy. For example, and I think this came up in our discussion about these different metrics, the experience of being over or under assessed is simply different depending on who you are. A wealthy homeowner of a being over assessed is that's more likely to be experiences and inconvenience. You have a lot of means you're like I have to pay a little bit extra. Maybe I don't buy you know this fancy coat or something that I wanted because I'm paying more but it's not a huge deal. Lower income homeowners are much more likely to experience it as catastrophic. leaf_chunk.audio: As I think we've had the big kind of takeaway that we've been demonstrating throughout entirety of today's lecture. A fair system almost certainly needs to be accurate in some demonstrable sense, but it's not necessarily fair. Fairness is about more than some way of quantifying the relationship between inputs and outputs of the system. All kinds of questions about fairness show up here too that might feel like they go beyond your responsibility as a data scientist. Is a neutral or flat tax rate really fair? Is it about a matter of policy rather than about how close your model reflects the policy? That's often not part of the decision space, the data scientist, but it is a larger political question that matters for the work that you do. Other levels of fairness are often occluded by hidden sort of made harder to see by excessively focusing on accuracy. For example, and I think this came up in our discussion about these different metrics, the experience of being over or under assessed is simply different depending on who you are. A wealthy homeowner of a being over assessed is that's more likely to be experiences and inconvenience. You have a lot of means you're like I have to pay a little bit extra. Maybe I don't buy you know this fancy coat or something that I wanted because I'm paying more but it's not a huge deal. Lower income homeowners are much more likely to experience it as catastrophic.

children.title: Social Effects of Assessment Accuracy and Fairness
children.summary: The meaning of assessment errors varies significantly, potentially impacting individuals' lives, such as losing a home. Optimizing metrics of accuracy must consider social effects and harms, necessitating engagement with affected individuals rather than relying solely on abstract data. The concept of home value is relational, influenced by sociotechnical systems, and fostering public trust through transparency in data and model sharing is crucial for equating accurate assessments with fairness.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Again, the same error for two different people has two very different meanings. It might mean losing a home. The numbers don't tell you that by themselves. Simply trying to optimize for any metric of accuracy isn't enough if you don't consider the specific social effects and harms from the perspective of those who actually feel them. That means actually talking to people, getting to know them and not simply looking at this field of abstract numbers before you, and trying to imprint some abstract notion of fairness onto it. In the sense a system might be as I've suggested more fair that air is on the side of overvaluing high value homes and air is on the side of undervaluing low value homes, even if it means reducing the overall accuracy of the model by making it more progressive again it's a political choice. It's about deep held sets of values about what you think is actually right in the world. Video: 1: 1. Question/Problem Formulation
Ari Edmundson

(more accurate*) than another?

children.title: Social Trust and Fairness in Assessments
children.summary: Excessive focus on accuracy in assessments can neglect broader justice aspects, particularly the cumulative impacts of historical discrimination. Fairness is an ongoing concern tied to a larger social system, and the CCAO seeks to enhance public trust through transparency in data and model decisions. Additionally, improving fairness in outputs requires an understanding of historical contexts and responsible data practices that emphasize self-reflection and collaboration.

children.title: Social Trust: Accuracy and Fairness in Assessments
children.summary: Excessive focus on accuracy can overlook broader aspects of justice, including the cumulative effects of historical discrimination. Fairness is not a momentary assessment but relates to a larger social system where discrimination compounds over time. The CCAO aims to build public trust through transparency, sharing data and model decisions to connect accurate assessments with fairness.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Well, excessively focusing on accuracy also can miss other aspects of justice that fairness don't always fully bring into light. You can miss the cumulative effects of all caught both historical and other forms of discrimination that can add up over time. Right, the whole history of being over assessed for many years, the system doesn't actually give you money back that you overpaid. Or it's about the accumulation and overlapping of different kinds of discrimination. Fairness is not something that just happens at one discrete moment in time. Oh, this is, you know, the algorithm is unfair. What's the larger social system? and how exactly does this relate to that whole constellation of different forms of discrimination. How do effects accumulate? How do they get compounded by the work that you might be doing? Video: 1: Social trust and translating accuracy* into fairness

children.title: Fairness, Transparency, and Historical Context in Data Practice
children.summary: Improving fairness in outputs does not rectify historical overassessment and discrimination. Fairness and transparency are context-dependent, necessitating an understanding of the historical context of problems, data, and social actors involved. Responsible data practice should expand beyond metrics optimization to include self-reflection on expertise and collaboration with others.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What are their effects not just on individuals but communities? Improving the fairness of outputs doesn't address years of overassessment and discrimination by other agencies. Two, fairness and transparency are context dependent. Again, for I think all the reasons that we've just been discussing, I don't think we have to explain this one any further. Three, one big takeaway is therefore, work with contexts. Figure out what's the historical context of the problem, the data, the social actors involved. What are the consequences? Part of the point of the human context at fixed program is widening the scope of what's considered part of responsible data practice. Beyond simply saying, okay, given some metrics that have been given to me, have I optimized for this particular form of relationship between inputs and outputs. That means doing things like check in your own expertise. What are the limits of the tools I bring to bear? Who else can I talk to to figure out whether I'm doing the right thing? children.title: Limits of Data Analysis and Social Trust
children.summary: Data analysis can assist in evaluating home values but is insufficient for addressing social issues, requiring a broader understanding of social dynamics. Transparency in data sharing is vital for building public trust, linking accurate assessments to fairness, and ensuring effective communication and social work to interpret data models.

children.title: Limits of Data Analysis in Social Contexts
children.summary: Data analysis can aid in assessing home values, but it is not always the right tool for addressing social issues. A broader toolkit and understanding of social dynamics are necessary for meaningful impact, emphasizing that better numbers alone won't reshape reality. Transparency in data sharing is crucial for fostering public trust and equating accurate assessments with fairness.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And lastly, always keep in mind, therefore, the power in limits of data analysis for solving social problems. As illustrated in this case to create a more accurate assessment of a home's value. Is that always the right tool? To a hammer, everything looks like a nail as the saying goes. The larger the toolkit you bring with you to take action on real-world problems, the greater the positive impact you'd be able to have. And so this means not assuming that simply putting just better numbers out of the world will magically reshape the world in their image. The numbers do work, but exactly what work they do involves social dynamics that goes beyond. The technical robustness of your inferences. And so that might just mean starting with a broader conception of the meaning of your question, what is a home worth? Video: 1: Social trust and translating accuracy* into fairness

children.title: Social Trust and Fairness in Data Models
children.summary: The discussion centers on the relationship between social trust and the perceived accuracy of data models, particularly regarding property assessments. The CCAO relies on transparency—sharing data and model results—to build public trust and link accurate assessments to fairness. It emphasizes that while numbers are expected to be self-explanatory, effective communication and social work are essential to interpret and utilize them properly.
